chunk_summary: Customer research is the backbone of all SaaS marketing. Without understanding your dream clients' problems, goals, and the exact words they use, your messaging and content will fall flat. Interview 7-10 recently won (or churned) clients to get unbiased, deep insights, then analyze transcripts for patterns to inform all marketing.
content: First up, we've got customer research. Now, you might be thinking, Sam, we've got AI tools that can do everything now. We can run surveys, we can pull insights, my sales team get recordings, and all this kind of stuff. The truth is, if you skip or neglect customer research, everything in your SAS marketing engine is going to fall flat. Solid customer research is the backbone of great B2B and SAS marketing because without it, your messaging, your marketing, your content, and much, much more all falls flat because it doesn't resonate with your dream clients problems, goals, and jobs to be done. If you skip this vital stage, nothing else really matters when it comes to your SAS marketing engine. So, my favorite way to do this is to interview seven to 10 recently won clients. The reason we want recently won clients is because they don't have a bias to you. Long-term customers are already going to really like you. They're going to like your service and product. They'll probably be way too biased and give skewed answers. So, you want new clients. Ideally, 7 to 10 is quite a decent range, more if you can, or if you've got some recently churned clients as well to throw into the mix, that can give some really useful and helpful data and insights, too. And remember, we want to dive deep to get the truth. We don't want fluffy answers. We want stuff that's really going to inform our go to market, our messaging, and our channels.
questions_on_this_chunk: Why is customer research crucial for SaaS marketing?
How many customers should I interview for research?
Should I interview new or long-term clients?
What kind of insights should I look for in customer interviews?

chunk_summary: The seven key customer interview questions: the exact problem before buying, what they tried in-house, when it became urgent, where they go for trusted information, competitors evaluated, what nearly stopped them from choosing you, and what they hate most about your industry. Record the calls and use AI to pull patterns and exact phrasing for your copy.
content: Here's seven of my favorite questions to ask for your customer interviews. And of course, you can tweak these, chop and change them depending on your industry and the nuances of your dream clients. I recommend running these sessions on a Zoom, Google Hangouts, or Riverside Call, and getting those recorded with an AI call recorder. So then you can go through the transcripts and pull the golden nuggets out at the end which we'll go through in a sec. All right. So the seven questions that we recommend are what was the exact problem that you were experiencing before reaching out to our team? What did you do in house to try and fix that expensive problem? When did it become so annoying, so frustrating that you decided to start evaluating solutions to solve the issue? Where do you go to when it comes to getting trusted information to evaluate or look out for new solutions, vendors or software? As well as us, what other competitors did you evaluate in the process? What made you nearly not choose us? Perhaps you on the fence for a little while and what eventually was that tipping point where you decided it made sense to work with us? Or of course, if they didn't decide to work with you, could you be as blunt as possible and share all the reasons why you felt we weren't a good fit? And lastly, I love this one. What do you hate most about our industry?
questions_on_this_chunk: What questions should I ask in a customer interview?
How do I get honest feedback about why someone didn't buy?
What tool should I use to record customer interviews?
How can I use customer interviews to find my headline?

chunk_summary: How to use research insights: pull transcripts from recorded calls into ChatGPT to spot patterns and frequently used phrases. Use the exact words clients use instead of jargon. This informs messaging, website copy, ads, videos, and channel selection. Make research ongoing — not a one-time project — to stay aligned with the market.
content: Once you've recorded those sessions with recently won or recently churned customers or a mix of all the above, then what you want to do is pull all those transcripts from the call. Run those through a tool like chat GPT or similar. And what you want to do is look for the patterns. Look for the things that are most frequently said for each question. So you can start pulling golden nuggets out to inform your messaging. And that could be on your website, on your ads, on your videos, on your media, on your social posts, and more. It can also help inform the channels that you use because you know where your dream clients are actually going to get trusted information. And it can also help you understand the actual words your dream clients use to describe their problems, their jobs to be done, their goals, their motivations. So instead of talking in jargon and using really crazy words like cutting edge synergy allin-one just this random Sash jargon that companies love to use, you're actually using the words that come out of your dream clients mouths consistently. So you're way more likely to resonate with them. ... This shouldn't be a one and done. We want to constantly collect feedback from our dream clients, target prospects, and more so we know exactly what they care about. We can keep hitting home when it comes to our messaging and our go to market. At Breaking B2B, we do this in a number of ways. So, I run a podcast called Breaking B2B where I interview SAS marketing leaders each and every week to keep a strong pulse on what SAS marketing leaders actually care about and how they're driving growth today. Each month I hop on calls with our customers to understand what they're struggling with, how we can be helping them further, what channels are working, what's not, what they think of the market, so I can keep a good general pulse on the market when it comes to SAS.
questions_on_this_chunk: How do I analyze customer interview transcripts?
What should I do with the exact phrases customers use?
How often should I do customer research?
Can a podcast help with ongoing customer research?

chunk_summary: SEO and AEO (AI Engine Optimization): you must appear in Google organic search and LLMs like ChatGPT when prospects are comparing vendors, frustrated with competitors, or ready to buy. Focus on 'money keywords' — low search volume but very high sales intent terms like 'competitor X alternative', 'best X software'. These rank faster and convert better than informational content.
content: Next up, we got SEO and AEO. In 2026 and beyond, you need to be showing up top of Google organic search and LLMs like Chat GPT. When your dream clients are ready to speak to sales and are searching directly for your software or solution, when they're comparing vendors, or when they're frustrated with their existing vendor, or if they're looking to solve an expensive problem. If you don't show up top, you're missing out. Your competitors are getting a free lunch and they're stealing all that mind share, all that traffic, and all those inbound leads, signups, and demos. ... But sadly, many SAS companies approach SEO completely wrong. They think they need 12 months minimum to see any results ... But in my experience, many SAS companies can drive inbound signups, leads, and demos as quick as 90 days with the right focus strategy. Here's our exact approach at breaking B2B when we work with SAS companies. First, we want to identify what are your money keywords or money prompts. I.e., what does a dream client of yours search for on Google organic search or an LLM? Not when they're information gathering or messing around, but when they have high sales intent to speak to a sales rep, to take a trial, or to invest in your solution today, or when they're comparing vendors, frustrated with their existing vendor, or looking to solve an expensive problem. So, think of searches like competitor alternative, competitor 1 versus competitor 2, best x software, bestex tools, bestex platform, or someone that's looking to solve a very specific problem or needs a very specific integration. For example, often times these money keywords have way lower search volume in terms of monthly traffic, but the sales intent is way higher, so they're way more likely to drive demos, and they're often lower keyword difficulty. So, it means they can rank way faster.
questions_on_this_chunk: What are money keywords in SEO?
How can SaaS companies rank in ChatGPT?
Why should I target low-volume keywords?
Can SEO results be achieved in 90 days?

chunk_summary: SEO case study — RB2B: a visitor identification SaaS at $6.4M ARR. Strategy: identify money keywords (e.g., 'leadfeeder alternatives'), build 'best of' listicles positioning own product as #1 backed by customer proof, then do fair comparisons. Earn niche-relevant backlinks. Results: #1 rankings on Google and ChatGPT for money keywords, 50% traffic increase, 201% growth in organic signup page hits.
content: Here's a real example. One of our clients, RB2B, they're a visitor identification SAS. They help you identify visitors of your website at the human at the person level and flag them up in Slack so your sales team can reach out and start nurturing and outreaching those prospects. They're around 6.3 6.4 million ARR. We're helping them scale up their SEO and their AAO engine with a focus on driving signups, pipeline, and revenues. So we've recently got them organic number one in both Google organic search as well as chat GBT and LLM for a bunch of money keywords that are driving signups pipeline and revenue. So things like best website visitor identification software we can see them number one and then competitor alternative terms. So lead feeder a big competitor to them. So if someone's perhaps frustrated with lead feeder or maybe looking to move away or weighing up their options if they search lead feeder alternatives we can see RB2B also taking that top spot there. ... So a quick snapshot is first and foremost, we worked out what their money keywords and prompts were. ... Then, we build best-in-class pages that make sense, that match the search intent depending on what it was. So for example with lead feeder alternatives we know that typically Google organic as well as LLMs are a big fan of product listicicals. So we evaluate the top 10 lead feeder alternatives for 2026. ... you position your own software as number one but you back it by customer research. ... you leverage social proof, customer quotes, case study review videos. You show your product in action under the bonnet. ... And then we started earning DA50 plus solid niche relevant back links to juice up some of the pages that had a lot of competition. ... As a result of this work, RB2B is now ranking top for many of their money keywords and prompts. Traffic from these money keywords is up 50% and we've seen a 201% growth in organic signup page hits.
questions_on_this_chunk: How did RB2B use SEO to grow signups?
What type of page works for competitor alternative keywords?
How do you structure a product listicle to rank well?
What results can a SaaS expect from SEO on money keywords?

chunk_summary: Niche influencer partnerships: partner with founders or influencers who have your exact ICP as their audience (e.g., on LinkedIn). Do a reciprocal deal where you provide your service to them, and both parties document the journey publicly — sharing strategy, roadmap, ups and downs, and real-time results. This builds unbeatable trust because it's a live, transparent case study.
content: Next up, we've got niche influencer partnerships. So this has been an absolute gamecher for our business breaking B2B. In short, it's about partnering with folks that have your exact dream clients as their audience, ideally on platforms like LinkedIn, which is where a lot of our ICPs hang out, but yours might be another channel. So to give you a real life example of what we're doing right now in this space, we're partnering with two niche relevant influencers. So we're partnering with Adam Robinson, the founder over at RB2B and Adam Holgren, the founder over at Fiba. And in our case, both these folks have a strong following on LinkedIn of our ICP SAS marketing leaders and SAS founders. So, we're running their entire SEO and AEO engine, and all of us are documenting the full journey across LinkedIn, sharing the ups, the downs, the plan of actions, the results, the case studies, and more. ... So, how it works step by step is each month I'll do a long- form organic post on LinkedIn showing the full strategy for the month ahead in terms of the SEO and AEO work we're doing. ... We basically share a road map of this is exactly the plan of action. So to drive organic signups, leads, and demos as quick as humanly possible through SEO and AO. This is why we're doing it. This is the full road map. ... And we share live results and impact as we see them come in. And so it's basically a live case study that folks can follow along that they post about and we post about. So why is this so effective when the world we're in now when so much can be pretended and faked with AI. The trust it builds when a founder or an ICP is posting directly about working with you showing the ups, the downs, the results, the trust it builds is unbeaten.
questions_on_this_chunk: What are niche influencer partnerships in B2B SaaS?
How do I find influencers to partner with?
How do you structure a live case study partnership?
Why is documenting a client journey more trustworthy than a polished case study?

chunk_summary: YouTube and podcasting for SaaS: adopt a 'YouTube-first' approach with polished, evergreen playbooks, case studies, and how-tos. Embed these videos on your site for SEO uplift. Repurpose clips for LinkedIn. Podcasts with industry leaders work as ABM — many guests become customers or referrers. Both channels have a compounding flywheel effect over time.
content: At four, we've got YouTube and podcasting. When it comes to YouTube, so few B2B and SAS companies are nailing it. Many use YouTube as a dumping ground for random webinars, old content, and more instead of having that YouTube first video approach. Therefore, the playing field is wide open for SAS companies that want to take it seriously. At Breaking B2B, we share playbooks each and every week just like I'm doing now across SAS SEO, SAS marketing, AI search, AEO. We share our full case studies, playbooks, walkthroughs, guides, and more. exact step by steps of what's working today to drive pipeline through SEO. The good thing about this is the videos are evergreen and searchable both within YouTube. They can be pulled up in Google organic search and LLMs like chat GPT. This means the videos can drive trust, mind share, even inbound leads, demos or signups not just now but for months and years to come. Plus, we embed these YouTube videos, the guides, the playbooks, the how-tos, the walkthroughs, as well as the results across our landing pages, articles, our case studies, and more. ... And on the podcast side of things, the breaking B2B podcast, most weeks we'll interview SAS marketing leaders to sit down with them and share exactly how they're driving growth of their company today. ... it even works as a bit of an ABM and accountbased marketing play cuz a lot of these SAS marketing leaders are potential prospects. So some of those eventually become our customers, refer business to us or they share the episodes and the content after the fact which promotes our podcast to a wider network of more ICPs and dream clients. So it has this really strong evergreen and flywheel effect.
questions_on_this_chunk: How can SaaS companies use YouTube effectively?
Should I embed YouTube videos on my website?
How can a podcast help with account-based marketing?
What type of YouTube content works best for B2B SaaS?

chunk_summary: Founder brand on LinkedIn: do the opposite of boring industry content. Get attention with humor, memes, skits, man-on-the-street interviews, then link it back to your solution. Comment value-add takes on niche posts. Build in public — share revenue, wins, failures. Treat your LinkedIn profile as a landing page with clear offer and proof. Consistency and authenticity build trust.
content: Next up, we've got building a founder brand on LinkedIn. I think if you're ignoring building a founder brand on LinkedIn or a similar channel in 2026, you're just sleeping on building trust with your market, driving inbound leads, building partnerships, getting potential customers, and way more. ... The playbook I run for my company, Breaking B2B, in my personal brand on LinkedIn. I basically try to do the opposite of what most SEO agency founders are doing. I find a lot of B2B marketing and SEO content quite dull, dry, and boring. So, my strategy really is to get attention first from dream clients and then link it back to our solution and our offer. So that could be anything from posting memes, doing man on the street interviews, asking folks what they think SEO means, if AI killed SEO, how to rank top of Google, or how to rank top of chat GBT. It could be a funny video, like for example, a while back we did a Nickelback spoof video talking about how hard B2B marketers have it ... Or it could be other video skits, me dressing up as SEO man, or something completely different that we're doing as an experiment. And of course, we share strategies of what's working today when it comes to AI search and SEO, full playbooks and rundowns and how-tos and case studies and results ... As well as that, I try and comment on five or so relevant industry folks. That could be potential partners, potential ICPs, prospects, even competitors with more value ad focused approach. So maybe my two cents on their post or what we're seeing working today. ... Not only this, but building in public is a big thing I believe right now as part of founder brand. of breaking B2B. We're often sharing our revenue numbers, what's working, what's not, what we're doing to improve our agency ... And I suppose the last piece of the puzzle was treating your LinkedIn page as a landing page. Just making sure it clearly shares exactly what you do, how you help, why you has plenty of proof of results and makes it nice and easy for folks to click through to your website.
questions_on_this_chunk: How do I build a founder brand on LinkedIn?
What type of LinkedIn content gets attention in B2B?
Should I be funny or serious on LinkedIn?
What does building in public mean for a founder?

chunk_summary: LinkedIn ads strategy: use thought leader ads from the founder's profile to boost organic content with a clear CTA. Mix top-funnel (problems), mid-funnel (playbooks), and bottom-funnel (case studies) content. Retarget warm traffic from SEO, YouTube, and site visitors to stay top-of-mind. Even a small budget ($2K/month) can generate booked calls when prospects consistently see your value and proof.
content: And lastly, we've got LinkedIn ads. So, I say LinkedIn's getting tougher and tougher when it comes to organic reach. Therefore, thought leader ads are a solid bet. So if your founder or other execs, marketers, revenue leaders, ops leaders, tech leaders, whoever it might be, maybe your founder or a few folks within your organization that have a decent following of your ICP and get some okay reach when it comes to LinkedIn, perhaps it's worth considering running thought leader ads to their content. So maybe you can do a mix of topfunnel content like you might be experiencing these problems. This is one way to look at it. Maybe some midfunnel playbooks, examples, how-tos, etc. and maybe some bottom funnel stuff like case studies, clients results, client review videos, and more. Then I'd recommend editing the post you want to run thought leader ads, too. So, make sure you've got a call to action link at the bottom of the post. Maybe that sends them to a landing page or your website or otherwise so they can take some kind of action. And then what we do at breaking B2B is we retarget all our traffic. So, we target our ICPs that have come in through SEO or maybe a flick to the website through Google Ads or flick to the site through YouTube or otherwise. so we can stay top of mind with those potential prospects as well as the leads in the pipeline. Now, plus we have a list of cold ICPs that we run these ads to. Where I really see the gold and the value here is if you're consistently shown to ICPS to those warm prospects that are in the pipeline now, but maybe not ready to buy just yet, but they constantly see helpful tips, playbooks, advice. Maybe you're doing stuff a little bit different, like I said, so you're getting their attention, but you're linking it back to useful and helpful educational content. ... And we've got a small budget right now of around 2K USD a month on LinkedIn ads, but we're still seeing booked calls come in on a steady basis that mention they found us or saw us through LinkedIn.
questions_on_this_chunk: What are thought leader ads on LinkedIn?
How should I structure a LinkedIn ads funnel?
Should I retarget website visitors with LinkedIn ads?
What results can I expect from a small LinkedIn ads budget?
